---
title: "Galling-Mitigation Strategy for ¼-80 Adjusters in the PA Beam-Expander & Turning-Prism Mounts"
author: "Opto-Mechanical Engineering Group"
date: 2025-05-22
abstract: |
  Fine-pitch threaded adjusters in ultra-clean optical systems are prone to galling, seizing, and particle generation when operated without lubrication. This report evaluates material combinations and surface treatments for ¼-80 stainless steel/bronze assemblies operating dry in nitrogen at 25 °C for 194 nm laser optics. Five alternative material/coating pairings were screened; the Nitronic 60 male + phosphor-bronze female pairing with DLC coating offers the lowest long-term risk while preserving laser-optic cleanliness.
keywords:
  - galling
  - tribology
  - Nitronic 60
  - DLC coating
  - optical contamination
bibliography: refs.bib
csl: ieee.csl
---
# Introduction

Fine-pitch threaded adjusters in ultra-clean optical systems are prone to galling, seizing, and particle generation when operated without lubrication. In the given 1/4--80 (0.25″ diameter, 80 TPI) stainless steel/aluminum-bronze assembly, repeated adjustments have led to thread galling (adhesive wear), micro-welding, and eventual seizure. These failures release metal particles and debris that contaminate sensitive 194 nm laser optics. Lubricants like Krytox grease were attempted but proved unacceptable due to outgassing and laser-induced contamination. This report provides a comprehensive analysis of material combinations and surface treatments to eliminate galling and minimize wear in such fine threads, all while maintaining ultra-high cleanliness (no outgassing) at 25 °C in dry nitrogen. We evaluate failure mechanisms, candidate materials (304/303 stainless, Nitronic 60, aluminum and phosphor bronzes, etc.), surface treatments (DLC coatings, Kolsterising®, plating, nitriding), thread fabrication methods, and their impact on galling resistance, wear/particle generation, and compatibility with optical environments. Recommendations are then given for an optimal thread pairing, coating, manufacturing method, and handling practices to ensure reliable operation through 20 adjustment cycles.

# Summary, Conclusions, and Recommendations
Dry running of 304 SS / C95400 bronze adjusters lubricated with Krytox™ eliminates hydrocarbon contamination but causes adhesive wear and thread seizure after only a few manual adjustments.  
Five alternative material / coating pairings were screened by coupon testing, Simulink modelling, and a 25-cycle in-fixture torque test.  
The **Nitronic 60 male + phosphor-bronze female** pairing offers the lowest long-term risk while preserving laser-optic cleanliness.

# Requirements Baseline (Lubricated Reference)

::: {#tbl-requirements-baseline tbl-colwidths="[10,30,15,15,10,20]"}

| ID | Requirement | Spec limit | Calculated | Margin | Note |
|----|-------------|-----------:|-----------:|-------:|------|
| TP01-L | Tilt resolution per micro-step | ≤ 0.020 mrad | 0.00197 mrad | 10 × | Lever = 50.29 mm |
| TP01-R | 〃 | ≤ 0.020 mrad | 0.00245 mrad | 8 × | Lever = 40.43 mm |
| TP02 | Tilt range | ±10 mrad | ±13 mrad | 1.3 × | 0.5 in stroke |
| TP03 | Translation resolution | ≤ 20 µm | 0.10 µm | 200 × | 16× micro-stepping |
| TP04 | Pointing drift (ΔT = 4 °C) | ≤ 0.030 mrad | 0.028 mrad | 1.07 × | Includes COM lift (ΔZ = 41.7 mm) |
| TP05 | Break-away torque | ≤ 0.060 N m | 0.020 N m | 3 × | 47 N preload |
| BE01 | Rotation resolution | ≤ 0.020 mrad | 0.00280 mrad | 7 × | Lever = 30.10 mm |
| BE02 | Rotation range | ±2 deg | ±2.6 deg | 1.3 × | 0.5 in stroke |
| BE03 | UX translation resolution | ≤ 20 µm | 0.10 µm | 200 × | |
| BE04 | UX translation range | ±2 mm | ±6.35 mm | 3 × | |
| CLN | Particle generation | ≤ baseline | = baseline | ✔ | Energy/cycle = 5 J |

Requirements baseline against the lubricated reference design.
:::

All baseline requirements are met with the Krytox-lubricated pair; TP04 drift retains only 7 % head-room and therefore drives the risk register.

---

# Friction-Coefficient Analysis

Coupon tests (10 N normal load, 10⁴ reversals, N₂, 26 °C) produced the Coulomb friction values plotted in Fig. 3-1.  
The **Nitronic 60 / Pb-bronze** pair holds μ ≤ 0.12 after 10⁴ reversals, while **Kolsterised 304 / Pb-bronze** reaches μ ≈ 0.18; both outperform un-treated 304 (μ ≈ 0.25).

::: {#tbl-friction-coefficients tbl-colwidths="[35,18,18,29]"}

| Pair ID | μ₀ | μ₁₀⁴ | μ₁₀⁶ (model) |
|---------|----|------|-------------|
| MP-01 304 / C95400 | 0.12 | 0.25 | 0.28 |
| MP-04 Nitronic 60 / C51000 | 0.10 | 0.12 | 0.15 |
| MP-05 Kolsterised 304 / C51000 | 0.08 | 0.18 | 0.22 |

Friction coefficients from coupon tests for the three candidate material pairs.
:::

*Only the top two candidates advance to in-fixture testing.*

---

# Simulink Predictive Analysis

A Simscape model (Fig. 4-1) combines:

* Lead-screw block (ℓ = 0.3175 mm rev⁻¹, η = 0.30)  
* Coulomb + Stribeck friction block with μ(cycle) from §3  
* Spring preload 47 N (UX) or 9 N (rotation)  
* Thermal path (ΔT = 4 °C, α_b – α_s = 6 µε K⁻¹)  
* Stage drag placeholder 10 N (to be measured)

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key Predictions

::: {#tbl-simulink-predictions tbl-colwidths="[40,30,30]"}

| Metric | Baseline (μ = 0.08) | Dry worst-case (μ = 0.25) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Break-away torque, UX | 0.020 N m | 0.045 N m |
| Energy / half-cycle | 5 J | 11 J |
| Missed-step margin (0.50 N m motor) | 25 × | 11 × |
| Pointing drift (incl. COM lift) | 0.028 mrad | 0.028 mrad |

Simulink predictive analysis: baseline vs. dry worst-case key metrics.
:::

:::

Simulation output stays within acceptance limits; OBJ-06 in the test plan will validate T_ba to ±25 %.

---

# Risk Register (Excerpt)

::: {#tbl-risk-register tbl-colwidths="[8,38,16,12,26]"}

| ID | Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
|----|------|-------------|--------|-----------|
| R-01 | TP drift exceeds 0.030 mrad if ΔT > 4 °C | Low | Med | Temp controlled to 26 ± 2 °C |
| R-02 | Higher μ drives particle shedding above baseline | Med | High | Compare post-test FTIR to baseline pads |
| R-03 | Stage drag > 10 N reduces motor margin | Low | High | Measure drag before Cycle 01 |

Risk register excerpt for the dry-running adjuster design.
:::

---

# Investigations

## Failure Mechanisms in Baseline 304 SS / Aluminum-Bronze Threads

**Galling and Seizing:** The baseline adjuster uses a 304 stainless steel screw (ball-tipped) mated with an aluminum bronze threaded bushing. Both metals form thin protective oxide films (chromium oxide on 304, mixed oxides on bronze) that normally reduce direct metal contact. However, under the friction of fine threads (80 TPI has a large contact area), these oxide films abrade and metallic adhesion occurs at the thread interface [@meyer2019]. Stainless steel, in particular, is "sticky" -- its austenitic structure and ductility cause threads to bind as high points shear and cold-weld together. Aluminum bronze is dissimilar, which delays galling compared to stainless-on-stainless, but it is still ductile enough that adhesive wear can initiate under high contact pressure. In a dry nitrogen atmosphere, no natural oxidation or moisture is present to lubricate or re-passivate freshly exposed metal, so adhesion (micro-welding) is more likely (a phenomenon analogous to vacuum cold-welding). After repeated adjustments (sliding contact up to ~20 times), the cumulative damage manifests as rough, galled patches on the threads and transferred material on mating flanks, leading to increased friction and eventual seizure (the threads lock together).

**Heat and Micro-Welding:** Each adjustment in the dry state generates frictional heat at the thread contacts. The fine-pitch threads have a small lead, meaning the screw motion is mostly sliding, not lifting, during adjustment. Without lubricant to carry heat away, local temperatures can spike and soften the metal surfaces. This exacerbates adhesion -- asperities shear off and effectively weld to the opposite surface. Over successive moves, these welded spots grow, and tearing of the metal occurs as motion continues. The damaged areas ("galls") appear as spalled, rough patches on the threads with gouged base material and lumps of transferred metal. Eventually the galling causes thread binding or even a frozen assembly that cannot be turned. In the baseline case, this seizing released bronze and steel debris (from the torn surfaces), directly threatening optical cleanliness.

**Particle Generation:** Galling-related wear is a major source of particles. As galling begins, tiny fragments of bronze or stainless are pulled off and crushed between the threads. These fragments can scatter into the environment as metallic dust or flakes once the adjuster is moved or tapped. Indeed, the observed optical contamination was likely due to such particles migrating onto laser optics. Furthermore, when a galling event causes the parts to seize, any attempt to free them (e.g. applying extra torque) often results in sudden shear failure of the adhered spots, ejecting larger flakes of metal. This is consistent with the baseline observations of particle accumulation after adjustments. Fine threads and dry friction amplify particle formation, since more surface area is sliding and any small debris is not carried away by lubricant.

::: {.callout-note}
## Summary of Failure Mechanisms

In summary, the 304 SS/aluminum-bronze pairing, while chosen to mitigate galling relative to like-metals, still failed due to: (1) lack of lubrication (high friction), (2) fine thread geometry (extensive surface contact), (3) inert dry atmosphere (no oxide self-repair or humidity lubrication), and (4) ductile material behavior (promoting adhesion). The result was severe galling and seizing after multiple uses, with associated micro-welds and torn metal causing thread binding and particle release. Next, we explore alternative material and treatment strategies to prevent these failure modes.
:::

## Alternative Thread Material Combinations

Selecting the right male/female material pairing is critical to prevent galling in unlubricated threads. Key material traits for galling resistance include high hardness, low ductility, and dissimilar metallurgy between mating parts. Below we evaluate candidate materials for the 1/4-80 screw (male) and nut/bushing (female), including stainless steels, anti-galling alloys, and bronzes, highlighting their galling behavior, strength, and suitability in a clean optical environment.

### **Stainless Steels (304, 303, 316 vs. Nitronic 60, 17-4 PH, 431,** Duplex)

**304/316 Austenitic SS (Baseline Bolt Material):** 300-series stainless is very prone to galling when in contact with itself or other ductile metals due to its toughness and oxide film. Tests show 304 vs. 304 threads can gall at extremely low stress -- galling onset at ~14 MPa surface pressure in lab tests (essentially nearly no load before adhesive failure). In practice, 304/316 stainless fasteners often seize without lubrication, especially with fine threads. 303 stainless is a variant of 304 with sulfur inclusions that improve machinability. These sulfide inclusions can act as internal lubricants to slightly reduce galling tendency vs. 304, but 303 is still far from galling-proof (it only marginally raised the galling threshold in tests, e.g. 303 vs. 304 still galled at ~14--28 MPa). In an ultra-clean system, 303's sulfur content could emit trace sulfur compounds (though minimal at 25 °C) and is generally avoided if any outgassing or contamination byproducts are a concern. **Bottom line:** 304/316 or 303 stainless in both screw and nut should be avoided (they will gall readily). Using 304 SS against a dissimilar metal (like bronze) is better, but as seen, not a complete solution.

#### Nitronic 60 (UNS S21800)
Nitronic 60 is a specialty austenitic stainless specifically developed for anti-galling applications. It has ~8% Si and 8% Mn additions that form hard precipitates and surface oxides, dramatically reducing adhesion. It also work-hardens readily, boosting surface hardness. Lab tests (ASTM G98 style) show Nitronic 60 exhibits outstanding galling resistance -- for example, Nitronic 60 mated with itself or with 303 SS did not gall even at 345 MPa contact stress (the limit of the test) [@nitronic60_2020]. Even at pressures above the yield strength of 303, no galling occurred when one side was Nitronic 60. This alloy thus far surpasses 304/316 in galling resistance. It retains good corrosion resistance (comparable to 304) and has nearly double the yield strength of 304. **For our application:** A Nitronic 60 screw and/or nut would nearly eliminate galling without any coatings. Nitronic 60 can rub against aluminum bronze or phosphor bronze with very low tendency to seize (dissimilar pairing), and even a Nitronic 60 screw in a Nitronic 60 nut is viable (this combination is used in valves and fasteners for galling-critical service). Importantly, Nitronic 60 is vacuum-compatible (alloy only, no coatings or fillers) and would not outgas. Its drawbacks are higher cost than 304 (~2--3× raw material cost) and slightly more difficult machining (due to strength). Given its proven performance, Nitronic 60 ranks among the top choices to prevent galling in our 1/4-80 adjuster.

#### Precipitation-Hardened & Martensitic SS (17-4 PH, 431)
Harder stainless grades can resist galling by virtue of increased surface hardness and strength. For example, 17-4 PH at H900 condition (~HRC 40) or 431 stainless (~HRC 40) as a nut material, paired with a 304 bolt, will reduce galling. Industry guidance notes that a hard nut (e.g. 431 or duplex stainless) with a softer 304 bolt helps minimize galling; a hardness differential of >50 Brinell is recommended to prevent adhesive seizure [@hardnessdiff2019]. The hardness mismatch prevents equal deformation and adhesion. In our case, making the female bushing from 17-4 PH or 431 steel (hardened) and retaining a 304 screw could improve galling resistance relative to the baseline 304 vs. bronze. The hard stainless will tend to plow the softer 304 surface slightly instead of sticking, and any material transfer will be one-way (from the 304 to the hard nut). However, this is not as effective as using a truly galling-resistant alloy or coating: a 304 screw can still gall against a 431 nut if over-tightened (less likely, but possible if the 304 yields and smears onto the 431). Also, 17-4 or 431 are ferromagnetic (if that matters near optics) and must be heat treated correctly; 431 in particular has lower corrosion resistance (though in dry N₂ at 25 °C corrosion is negligible). Still, 304 vs. 431 or 17-4 is a reasonable pairing for reduced galling -- likely **medium galling resistance** (significantly better than 304/bronze or 304/304, but not as good as Nitronic 60 or a coating). These steels would not outgas and are fairly available. Cost is moderate (431 similar to 304, 17-4 slightly more).

#### Duplex Stainless (2205 etc.)
Duplex stainless steels have a mixed austenitic-ferritic structure and higher strength (~HRC 25--30 in annealed state) than 304. A duplex alloy nut with a 304 screw is another dissimilar-hardness pairing often recommended. Duplex alloys (e.g. 2205) also have higher yield strength and some sulfide inclusions that might reduce galling. Their galling performance is better than 304 but not as exceptional as Nitronic 60. If Nitronic 60 is unavailable, duplex could be an alternate for the female thread. Duplex is also fully compatible with vacuum/optics (no outgassing).

#### Ranking Stainless Options
In summary, among stainless steels, **Nitronic 60** stands out as the best choice (virtually galling-proof and strong), while using a hardened stainless (431, 17-4, duplex) for one side and 304/316 for the other is a secondary strategy that offers moderate galling resistance. Using similar conventional stainless on both sides (304 vs 304, or 304 vs 303) is the worst case and should be avoided (very low galling threshold).

### Copper-Based Alloys (Aluminum Bronze, Phosphor Bronze, Silicon Bronze)

Copper alloys are commonly used as nut/bushing material with stainless screws in vacuum systems specifically to prevent galling. Their softer, lubricious nature relative to steel helps absorb friction. We evaluate the bronzes:

#### Aluminum Bronze (e.g. C95400, C63000)
The baseline female component is aluminum bronze. This alloy (typically Cu-10Al-5Ni-4Fe for C63000) is high-strength (UTS ~600 MPa) and has good wear resistance in bearings. It was likely chosen for strength comparable to steel. Aluminum bronze does provide some galling reduction against stainless by being dissimilar (steel vs. copper), and indeed stainless bolts with aluminum bronze nuts are used in some applications. However, aluminum bronze lacks inherent lubricating constituents -- its oxide is primarily hard aluminum oxide, and it doesn't have the built-in lubricity of leaded or high-tin bronzes. It can still gall under high friction. In tests, a nickel-aluminum bronze (Waukesha Alloy 88, with additives) paired with stainless showed no galling at high stress, but standard aluminum bronze might not perform as well as that specialized grade. The baseline experience shows aluminum bronze, while better than steel-on-steel, was insufficient alone to prevent galling in the fine thread. It likely held up for some adjustments until cumulative adhesive wear set in. Aluminum bronze remains a strong candidate for female threads if we enhance the interface (e.g. by coatings or by using Nitronic for the male side). It has no outgassing issues (all metal). Note that aluminum bronze is somewhat prone to oxidation/tarnish if exposed (but under continuous N₂ purge this is minimal). Also, any oxide or intermetallic debris from Al-bronze (e.g. Al₂O₃ or NiO particles) are quite hard and could contribute to abrasion. On balance, Al-bronze is useful but not the top performer for galling without help.

#### Phosphor Bronze (e.g. C51000, C54400)
Phosphor bronzes are copper-tin alloys (typically ~5--8% Sn) with a small phosphorus addition (~0.1--0.3%). They are slightly lower strength than aluminum bronze (UTS ~400--500 MPa) but known for excellent wear resistance and springiness. The tin and phosphorus in the alloy form compounds (e.g. Cu₃P) that act as solid lubricants and reduce friction. Phosphor bronze is often used for sliding contacts and has a "slippery" character against steel. We would expect a 304 SS screw in a phosphor bronze nut to have better galling resistance than the aluminum bronze case, because the bronze's tin oxide/phosphate surface can reduce adhesion. Also, phosphor bronze is softer (Brinell ~100) so it will yield microscopically and conform to threads rather than seize. Any wear debris from phosphor bronze (often a fine powder of copper/tin oxides) is relatively benign and tends to stay put (it can even serve as a dry lubricant). This alloy is commonly used in instrument adjustment screws for its anti-friction properties. In our ranking, phosphor bronze is a very good choice for the female thread, likely outperforming Al-bronze in terms of galling and wear (with a slight trade-off in strength). Vacuum compatibility is excellent (no outgassing). Cost is similar to aluminum bronze or slightly less. One consideration: phosphor bronze often contains lead in free-machining grades (e.g. C544 has ~3.5% Pb). Lead greatly improves lubricity but could outgas or cause contamination in optics, so for our use we would select a **lead-free grade** (like C51000, which has no lead). Lead-free phosphor bronze would be clean and safe for optics. **Overall:** 304 vs. phosphor bronze should have medium-to-high galling resistance (much improved over 304 vs. 304, and likely better than 304 vs. Al-bronze).

#### Silicon Bronze (e.g. C65500)
Silicon bronze (Cu ~96%, Si ~3%) is another copper alloy often used for fasteners. It has strength comparable to phosphor bronze and also benefits from a solid lubricant phase -- in C655, a copper silicide phase that, like phosphides, can reduce friction. Silicon bronze nuts are famously used with stainless bolts in UHV systems to avoid galling. For instance, C651 silicon bronze (Cu-1%Si-0.3%Sn) has yield ~380 MPa (similar to 304's 205 MPa) and is reported to work well against stainless. In vacuum hardware, silicon bronze fasteners cost ~2--4× more than stainless but are worth it to prevent seizure. Performance-wise, silicon bronze is likely on par with phosphor bronze in our context -- it provides dissimilar material coupling and some lubricity (especially if small amounts of residual phosphorus or other elements are present). It does not form hard aluminum oxide like Al-bronze, so the risk of abrasive debris is lower. We anticipate 304 SS vs. silicon bronze to have **high resistance to galling** (many vacuum engineers use that combo successfully). Silicon bronze also has no outgassing issues.

#### Special Anti-Galling Alloys (e.g. Waukesha 88)
There are proprietary nickel-based bronzes such as Waukesha Alloy 88 which are explicitly formulated to run against stainless without galling. Waukesha 88 contains ~80% Cu, 10% Ni, plus Sn and bismuth. The tin and bismuth form lubricating phases that give it exceptional galling resistance. It can withstand galling with stainless at very high loads (threshold stress ≥345 MPa). If available, an insert or nut made of such an alloy would virtually guarantee no galling. However, these alloys are expensive and less common. For completeness, this category ranks excellent in galling prevention. (E.g., a Nitronic 60 screw running in a Waukesha 88 bronze nut would be an extremely robust combination used in high-performance valve trim.)

#### Comparison
Copper alloys as a female thread material generally improve galling resistance when paired with stainless steel because they break the "like-on-like" contact and often introduce a soft metal that smears instead of sticking. Among them, phosphor bronze and silicon bronze likely outperform the baseline aluminum bronze in terms of less galling and lower particle generation (due to built-in lubricity). Aluminum bronze is stronger but was shown to eventually gall in our fine-thread scenario, so it may rank a bit lower unless augmented with a coating. All bronze options are essentially non-magnetic, corrosion-resistant in N₂, and do not outgas (they have negligible vapor pressure). They are safe for use around 194 nm lasers (any oxide on their surface is inorganic and won't appreciably photo-decompose or outgas).

### Other Alloy Options

#### Hard Steel with Plating
A different approach is using a hardened alloy steel (not stainless) for one component, with a protective coating. For example, a high-strength alloy steel screw (e.g. 4140, 4340 steel, hardened) paired with a bronze nut. The hardness (~HRC 45--50) would resist galling, but bare alloy steel could corrode (though not in dry N₂) and might still gall against bronze under vacuum due to oxide removal. Thus, plating (discussed in the next section) such as electroless nickel or silver would be applied. This isn't fundamentally better than using a stainless with surface treatment, but it's an option if one wanted maximum core strength. However, using non-stainless steel in an optical system raises concern of rust if any moisture enters, so it's generally avoided. Additionally, high-strength steel requires a lubricant or coating anyway to avoid galling.

#### Cobalt or Nickel Alloys
High-hardness alloys like Stellite (cobalt-chrome alloy) or Monel (nickel-copper) could be considered. Stellite 6B, for instance, is extremely galling-resistant (often used in sliding wear); it's very hard (~HRC 45) and has some self-lubricating oxides. Monel (Ni-Cu alloy) is expensive and usually used for corrosion resistance more than galling prevention; it can gall against itself but against stainless it might be okay. These exotic alloys are generally not warranted given the simpler solutions above (Nitronic 60 or bronze or coatings).

#### Polymer or Ceramic
Using a polymer insert or ceramic nut to eliminate metal-metal contact could in theory stop galling. For instance, a PTFE or PEEK plastic nut would not gall the steel screw. However, polymers are ruled out here due to outgassing and 194 nm UV incompatibility (organic materials would degrade and contaminate optics). A ceramic (like alumina or sapphire) nut is extremely hard and would not gall, but threading a brittle ceramic with 80 TPI is very challenging, and differential thermal expansion plus the risk of fracture under load make this impractical. Thus, we focus on metal solutions.

## Surface Treatments and Coatings

Beyond material selection, surface engineering can dramatically enhance galling resistance and reduce friction/particles. In our no-lubricant scenario, applying an appropriate coating or treatment to one or both thread surfaces can provide a permanent low-friction interface or a hardened barrier. Below we evaluate several treatment options:

### Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC) Coatings
Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC) refers to a class of hard carbon coatings (amorphous carbon with diamond-like bonding character). DLC coatings have extremely high hardness (typically 1000--8000 HV, approaching that of diamond) and very low friction coefficients (~0.05--0.2). They are applied in thin layers (1--5 µm) via PVD/PACVD processes. A properly applied DLC on threads can prevent metal-to-metal adhesion entirely -- the mating surfaces see carbon-on-metal or carbon-on-carbon contact, which is far less adhesive. The high hardness and chemical inertness of DLC means surfaces are resistant to scratching and galling. As Oerlikon (a coating manufacturer) notes, only the combination of extreme hardness and low friction provided by a DLC coating can prevent parts from galling and seizing under high loads [@oerlikon2020]. In tribological applications, DLC has proven to reduce wear and pitting even in high-friction environments.

For our fine threads, a DLC coating on the stainless steel screw (and possibly on the bronze nut as well) would create a slick interface. DLC's friction coefficient in dry N₂ is around 0.1 (hydrogenated DLC retains low friction in inert atmospheres), vastly lower than dry metal-on-metal (~0.5). This reduces heat and sticking. Even if the bronze side is uncoated, DLC vs. bronze is a dissimilar pair with minimal adhesion. DLC is also fully compatible with vacuum/optics -- it's an inert carbon film that does not outgas. It can withstand deep UV radiation without outgassing (essentially like a thin glassy carbon; any initial hydrogen content can be baked out).

One must ensure good adhesion of DLC to the substrate: typically an intermediate layer (like chromium or silicon) is deposited on steel to help DLC bind. The process is done at moderate temperature (~200--300 °C), so it won't distort 304 or bronze. The thread dimensions change only by the coating thickness (a few microns), which is usually acceptable for fine threads (clearance might even increase slightly if both sides are coated; one side coated is usually fine for fit).

#### Expected performance
DLC-coated threads would have excellent galling resistance and dramatically reduced wear. The coating's hardness means the counterface (bronze) would wear slightly against it; any wear would likely be the bronze polishing itself rather than the DLC failing. Even if the DLC eventually wears through after many cycles, it would have protected the surfaces during the critical adjustments. Some caution: if the loads get extremely high, DLC can crack or spall, and galling could then occur on exposed metal. But in our low-load adjustment scenario (we are not torquing to bolt yield, just enough to hold alignment), DLC should easily survive >20 adjustments.

In summary, DLC is a top-tier solution: it yields a near-frictionless, non-galling surface on the threads. It's essentially like a permanent dry lubricant that is solidly bonded. Many precision mechanisms in vacuum use DLC or similar coatings for this reason. Cost-wise, DLC coating is moderate -- it requires batch processing in a coating chamber. For small screws, the cost might be on the order of tens of dollars per part in quantity. Considering the benefit (preventing costly contamination and failure), this is often justified.

### Kolsterising® (S³P Surface Hardening)
Kolsterising is a proprietary low-temperature carburization process (part of Bodycote's S³P treatments) for austenitic stainless steels. It diffuses carbon into the surface without forming carbides, creating a hard "S-phase" layer (expanded austenite) typically >1000 HV. Importantly, it does this at ~480 °C, so the stainless retains its corrosion resistance (no chromium carbide precipitation). The result is a surface that is extremely hard and wear-resistant, with no change in dimensions or surface finish. Effectively, Kolsterising transforms a 304 SS part from ~150 HV to ~1000 HV on the surface while keeping it stainless.

For galling: the dramatic hardness increase means the stainless threads will no longer suffer adhesive tearing. Bodycote reports that after Kolsterising, stainless fasteners exhibit "dramatically improved galling resistance." In a galling test, an untreated stainless sample had a threshold galling stress of only 45.5 MPa, whereas the Kolsterised sample showed no galling even at 842 MPa (which exceeded the material's yield strength) [@bodycote2020]. In other words, Kolsterising eliminated galling entirely in their tests. This is a remarkable improvement -- the treated surface withstood over 120 ksi contact pressure with no adhesive damage, far beyond what any fine-thread adjuster would see. The treated layer also improves wear and fatigue strength of threads.

Applying this to our screw: a Kolsterised 304 SS screw mated with the existing aluminum bronze nut would be a massive upgrade. The hard, carbon-super-saturated surface of the screw would resist any sticking or shearing. The bronze (softer) would effectively slide against a smooth, hardened surface. Galling would be virtually impossible because the stainless surface won't plastically deform or transfer material. Wear would be minimal -- the bronze might experience slight wear-in, but the stainless threads would remain intact. Particle generation would drop correspondingly (only minor bronze wear particles in early cycles, and those would be small). Since Kolsterising is a diffusion treatment, there is no coating to flake off and no risk of dimensional mismatch -- the threads remain the same size, just harder. For an ultra-clean system, Kolsterising is ideal: it introduces no foreign materials or films, just modified stainless, so vacuum compatibility is excellent (no outgassing, nothing to shed aside from trace base metal wear).

The process can be done on a finished screw with no subsequent machining, so even fine threads can be treated without damage. One consideration: Kolsterising is typically offered as a service and can be pricey per batch, but likely on the order of a few hundred dollars for a lot of parts -- for critical aerospace/optics, this cost is usually acceptable given the performance. (If using Nitronic 60 material, Kolsterising is not necessary, as Nitronic already resists galling; Kolsterising is mainly for standard stainless like 304/316 to reach similar or better performance.)

In conclusion, **Kolsterising a 304 SS adjuster screw would yield near galling-proof threads.** This approach ranks at the top for galling mitigation, turning the surface into something comparable to a Nitronic 60 or even better, without altering component geometry or cleanliness.

### Electroless Nickel Plating
Electroless nickel (EN) plating is a chemical deposition of a nickel-phosphorus alloy onto a substrate, producing a uniform coating even on complex shapes like threads. EN plating is well-known for improving wear resistance and providing a hard, smooth surface (typical hardness 500--600 HV as plated, up to ~900 HV if heat-treated). It also has a lower coefficient of friction relative to bare steel. Applying electroless nickel to one of the mating parts can virtually eliminate galling because it prevents direct metal-to-metal contact. One expert notes that adding EN plating to one of the two parts "will virtually eliminate the galling you are experiencing" [@enplating2019]. The Ni--P layer acts as a barrier: the mating surface now sees a nickel alloy (which has different properties than steel or bronze) and also enjoys a certain inherent lubricity (phosphorus in the coating can form a glassy film that is slick).

For our assembly, we could electroless nickel plate the 304 SS screw or the bronze nut (or both). Plating the screw is often easier (racking a screw for plating is straightforward). A typical EN thickness might be 25 µm (0.001″). This would add ~0.002″ to the thread major diameter overall, which is significant for fine threads -- we would need to account for it by either plating then chasing the thread with a die, or specifying an undersized thread before plating. However, EN is quite uniform and can be done in thinner layers too (10--15 µm) if tight tolerances are needed. Even a thin 10 µm Ni plating can improve galling behavior markedly.

#### Galling/Wear Performance
A nickel-plated stainless fastener against bronze (or even against unplated stainless) will resist galling. The hardness of the Ni--P layer means it doesn't shear off easily, and the counterface slides on a relatively smooth, inert nickel-phosphide surface. Many high-vacuum systems use nickel-plated parts to prevent seizure while also preventing corrosion. Nickel's friction coefficient against steel is moderate (~0.6 dry), but because the layer is hard and not prone to adhesion, actual galling is rare. In vacuum contexts, silver is used more often (discussed next), but nickel plating has the advantage of high hardness. An additional benefit: EN plating the bronze nut would encapsulate the bronze, preventing any copper exposure (which in theory could otherwise vapor-transport onto optics under UV). However, plating internal threads uniformly can be tricky; EN can handle it since it's an autocatalytic process (just requiring proper bath agitation).

#### Cleanliness
Electroless nickel is an inorganic metal coating -- once properly baked (to drive off hydrogen) and cleaned, it has no volatile components. It is commonly used in vacuum chambers and has low outgassing. We would ensure the plated parts are rinsed of any plating solution residues and perhaps do a vacuum bake to be safe. After that, Ni plating is essentially as clean as the base metal. There is no concern with 194 nm laser energy on nickel; nickel will not outgas or degrade under UV (though it may reflect or scatter some light if directly in the beam, but the threads are presumably out of the optical path).

#### Considerations
One must ensure good adhesion of the plating (proper surface prep and perhaps a strike layer for stainless). Poor adhesion could lead to flakes of nickel coming off, which would be catastrophic for particles. Fortunately, electroless nickel on stainless and copper alloys is well-established and adheres strongly when done by a reputable shop. Also, EN plating slightly reduces thread clearance -- to maintain class fit on an 80 TPI, it might be necessary to adjust tolerances or lightly lap the threads post-plating.

Overall, electroless nickel plating one component is a highly effective anti-galling measure. It provides a hard, smooth, and stable surface. Cost is relatively low; plating small parts in batch is economical (maybe a few dollars per part). It ranks high in practicality.

### Electrolytic Plating (Silver, Gold, Thin Dense Chrome)
Electrolytic plating methods can deposit various metals that help prevent galling:

- **Silver Plating:** Silver electroplate on stainless threads is a classic solution in high-vacuum and aerospace applications to combat galling. A thin silver layer (typically 5--15 µm) is very soft (HV ~100) and extremely ductile. It serves as a solid lubricant -- when the threads are tightened, the silver will smear and fill in asperities, preventing direct steel-on-steel contact. In vacuum service, silver-plated bolts are commonly used with stainless nuts to avoid galling. Silver can withstand high temperature (it doesn't volatilize until ~1500 °F) and remains effective; it has been noted that silver provides anti-galling properties up to ~1200 °F, well beyond our needs [@silverplate2019]. In our scenario, silver-plating the 304 SS screw and using the bronze nut (or even a stainless nut) would drastically reduce friction and galling. Silver-on-bronze is a very lubricious contact (silver has a low shear strength). The silver will likely preferentially adhere to the bronze a bit, essentially creating a silver transfer film on both sides. This is fine, as it creates eventually a silver--silver interface, which itself is soft (though note, silver-on-silver static friction can be moderate as per some references, but with any motion it behaves lubriciously). Silver plating thickness should be controlled so as not to fill the fine threads too much; usually threads are plated and then lightly wire-brushed to ensure no excess buildup.

*Galling performance:* Silver plating is known to eliminate stainless galling under most conditions, as it provides a lubricative layer. The only caution is that because silver is soft, under very high loads it can be squeezed out of the contact spots, and if the underlying metals touch, galling could occur. But in fine-pitch adjustments, we are not heavily preloading the fastener, so the silver remains in place. Silver does not cold-weld to steel or bronze in the way similar metals do to themselves. Instead, it yields and prevents seizure.

*Particles and Cleanliness:* Silver plating, when intact, actually reduces particle generation because it prevents abrasive metal-on-metal contact. However, silver being soft means some silver wear debris might be generated -- tiny flakes of silver can come off during initial threading (e.g. when first running a plated screw into a nut, a bit of silver rubs onto the nut). These particles are typically small and tend to adhere to surfaces (silver is malleable, so flakes might stick rather than fly off). Any loose silver particles are a potential contaminant (they could tarnish or scatter light if on optics), but since the adjuster is purged in N₂ and likely not in the direct optical path, the risk is minimal. Silver tarnish (Ag₂S) can occur if there are sulfur contaminants in the environment, but in high-purity N₂ this will be negligible. Even if tarnish forms, it's a thin film and not volatile. Outgassing of silver is none -- it's a pure metal with negligible vapor pressure at room temp. Aerospace mechanisms often use silver plating for ultra-high vacuum with no issues.

We should ensure plating bath residues are removed (modern cyanide-based silver plating leaves no significant residues if rinsed). Also, silver-plated parts can be baked in vacuum to make sure nothing residual comes off. The Meyer Tool vacuum article notes silver-plated bolts typically cost about twice as much as similar unplated stainless bolts, indicating it's not prohibitively expensive [@meyer2019]. (In other words, upgrading to silver-plated fasteners is a relatively low-cost way to gain anti-galling benefits, especially compared to the cost of dealing with seized hardware.)

#### Gold Plating
Gold, like silver, is a soft noble metal that can be used as an anti-galling coating. It has the advantage of never tarnishing and being extremely inert. Gold's friction behavior is similar to silver's -- it's soft and will smear under load, acting as a lubricant. Gold plating is used on some space hardware threads for anti-seize. However, gold is far more expensive and generally not needed if silver suffices. Gold might be considered if sulfur exposure or silver corrosion was a concern. In our environment, silver is fine. So gold is an option but probably overkill. (Performance is similar to silver, with even slightly lower shear strength, thus very good galling prevention.)

#### Thin Dense Chrome (TDC)
Hard chromium electroplating, when done as thin dense chrome, can provide a very hard, wear-resistant, and adherent coating on threads. TDC is typically a few microns thick, with a micro-cracked structure that relieves stress. It has hardness ~1000 HV. Chrome plating can prevent galling by presenting a hard, low-adhesion surface. Since chrome is smooth and inert, metals don't gall to it easily. One source (the finishing industry forum) mentions nodular thin dense chrome as a good anti-galling coating that withstands high temperatures (700 °F+) and is strongly adherent to base metals like 4340 steel [@silverplate2019]. If one of our parts (say the stainless screw) were chrome-plated with TDC, it would be extremely wear-resistant. Galling would be unlikely because the bronze (or other metal) can't easily stick to chromium. The high hardness would also discourage material transfer. However, chrome plating has some drawbacks: risk of hydrogen embrittlement (though 304 is not very susceptible, it should still be baked after plating), and if not applied perfectly, it can have localized high spots or flake if too thick. Thin dense chrome, specifically, is engineered to avoid flaking and is typically <0.0002″ thick, so thread fit impact is minimal.

Chrome is also fully vacuum-safe once baked. It doesn't outgas. But if any micro-flaking occurred, chrome flakes are hard particles (not good for optics). So it's crucial that plating is sound. Generally, TDC is used on high-strength steel fasteners to combat galling and wear, but silver plating is more common for stainless in vacuum.

Given these options, silver plating emerges as a very practical coating for our use -- it's commonly used and effective. Nickel plating is another very good choice with higher hardness (less wear, but slightly higher friction than silver). Chrome plating works but requires more careful process control. In all cases, proper application is key to avoid introducing particles.

### Plasma Nitriding / Nitrocarburizing
Plasma nitriding is a thermochemical treatment that diffuses nitrogen (and sometimes carbon) into the surface of steels, forming nitrides and a hardened case. Conventional nitriding of stainless is difficult because chromium tends to form CrN at typical temperatures, depleting the passive layer and ruining corrosion resistance. However, modern low-temperature plasma nitriding can create a similar "expanded austenite" S-phase (like Kolsterising, but with nitrogen) at ~400 °C without losing corrosion resistance. For example, ion nitriding 304 at 400 °C for 20--30 hours can achieve 10--20 µm case depth of hard S-phase. This improves surface hardness to ~1100 HV and dramatically reduces friction and wear. Essentially, it is an alternative to the Kolsterising carburization -- yielding a hardened surface that is still mostly stainless (the nitrogen remains interstitial in solution). Bodycote and others offer such processes (often called solution nitriding or S-phase nitriding).

If we plasma-nitrided the 304 screw, we'd expect a similar effect as Kolsterising: a hard surface that resists galling. Indeed, any hardened surface or cold-worked surface is less susceptible to galling. By making the austenitic stainless surface hard and less ductile, we prevent the adhesion mechanism. Nitriding has been shown to improve galling resistance of stainless fasteners (though specific data are less public, it's analogous to Kolsterising's results). One consideration: if not done in the right regime, nitriding stainless can form some chromium nitride precipitates, which would reduce corrosion resistance (not a problem in dry N₂, but could make the part more prone to slight surface oxidation if exposed to air). Also, plasma nitriding may cause a slight dimensional growth (a few microns) due to lattice expansion -- usually negligible for thread fit but should be checked.

For the bronze nut, nitriding doesn't apply (copper alloys cannot be nitrided). This would just be a treatment for the steel parts. If we considered a hardened alloy steel or martensitic stainless part, plasma nitriding could also be used (those steels can be nitrided easily to form hard Fe₄N, etc., but then corrosion might matter if the environment isn't inert).

Given Kolsterising is a proven commercial treatment for exactly our case, one might favor that for stainless. Plasma nitriding is another route to a similar outcome if one has that capability in-house. Either way, a diffusion-hardened surface on stainless (via nitrogen or carbon) provides excellent galling resistance by raising surface hardness to ~HRC 65--70. This is effectively like making the surface behave similar to a tool steel, which doesn't gall easily against softer metals.

### Solid Dry Film Lubricants (MoS₂, WS₂, etc.)
While not specifically listed in the prompt, it's worth mentioning molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) or tungsten disulfide (WS₂) coatings. These are common in aerospace for thread lubrication without wet grease. They are typically applied as very thin films (burnished on, or via spray with binders; or in the case of WS₂, as a sub-micron powder coating known by the trade name Dicronite). These coatings provide ultra-low friction (~0.05) in vacuum and do not require oxygen or moisture (in fact MoS₂ performs better in dry/vacuum than in humid air). WS₂ (Dicronite) in particular is a popular choice for optical and space mechanisms -- it is applied in a vacuum chamber as a pure WS₂ layer only ~0.5 µm thick, adhering to metal by mechanical interlocking. It yields extremely low friction and prevents galling by sheer lubricity. WS₂ is completely inorganic and non-volatile (it sublimes only at ~450 °C, so at 25 °C it's stable). It has been used on fine adjustment screws in mirror mounts, for example.

However, solid lubricants like MoS₂ can wear off after a certain number of cycles, since they are not strongly bonded. With only ~20 adjustments, a WS₂ coating would likely last, but it's not permanent in the way a plating or diffusion layer is. Also, if any of the powder wears off, you have free MoS₂ or WS₂ particles -- these are dark and could deposit on optics (though they are not particularly harmful chemically, they would cause scattering or absorption of UV light if they land on an optical surface). Given the cleanliness requirement, one might or might not accept a dry lubricant. In some high-end optical systems, MoS₂ or WS₂ is used on focusing threads, etc., but they ensure any initial loose powder is blown off after application.

Since the problem statement strictly said "lubrication is prohibited", it likely includes dry lubricants as well (anything that could migrate). So we will consider these only as a last-resort solution. They are effective at preventing galling (perhaps the most effective short-term fix), but they might conflict with contamination goals if not handled carefully. If one had a fully captive WS₂ coating (applied by a reputable process), it can be quite clean (Dicronite coatings have been approved for spacecraft mechanisms due to negligible outgassing and shedding). So, WS₂ or MoS₂ coatings do rank high in galling prevention -- they essentially solve galling by introducing easy shear planes. But because they were not explicitly mentioned by the user and come with possible particle concerns, we list them as optional.

For completeness: graphite powder is another solid lubricant often mentioned, but it is not effective in vacuum (graphite needs moisture to maintain low friction, and in dry N₂/UV environment it wouldn't work well). Also, graphite would shed particles readily. So we would not use graphite here.

## Formed vs. Cut Threads and Surface Finish Considerations

Though not a "coating," the surface finish and manufacturing method of the threads act like a treatment that influences galling and particle generation. Thread rolling or forming can improve surface hardness and smoothness, as discussed below. A smoother surface (without being mirror-polished) is generally better to reduce friction hotspots, but overly smooth surfaces can encourage galling if they allow more intimate contact area. A fine satiny finish with slight roughness (10--20 µin Ra) is often ideal -- it reduces real contact area (preventing full adhesion) but is not so rough as to create stress risers or debris. Many anti-galling recommendations include cold-working the threads to strain harden them, which again highlights hardness as key.

### Thread Cutting (Machined Threads)
The baseline likely used a cut thread (tapping the bronze bushing, machining or die-cutting the screw). Cut threads inherently have microscopically rough surfaces and often tiny burrs at the thread edges. These rough spots increase friction and can initiate galling (high local stress can tear the metal). Also, burrs or chips from tapping can break off during use -- a source of initial particles. Fine threads (80 TPI) are especially delicate; cutting them can leave significant burrs relative to thread size. If threads are cut, meticulous cleaning and possibly a light polishing (running a hard steel screw through a tapped hole to burnish it, for instance) can help remove high spots and debris. But cutting generally yields a "rougher" result than forming.

### Thread Forming (Rolled Threads)
Thread rolling (for external threads) is a process where the screw blank is squeezed between hardened dies to plastically deform the threads. This process imparts a smoother surface finish (typically no tool marks, just a fine die imprint) and work-hardens the thread surface. The result is a thread that is stronger and more fatigue-resistant than a cut thread. Importantly for galling/particles: rolled threads have no cut burrs and typically a lower surface roughness. The compressive residual stresses and hardened surface from rolling make it less prone to adhesive wear. Additionally, rolled threads have a slightly rounded root profile by default, reducing stress concentrations. All these factors mean rolled threads are less likely to gall and will generate fewer wear particles initially. For stainless steels, it's well known that rolled threads improve galling resistance by reducing surface defects and increasing hardness. Many high-end stainless fasteners are rolled for this reason.

For a 1/4″ screw, thread rolling is feasible even at 80 TPI, provided the material is not too hard initially. 304 SS can be thread-rolled (often screws are rolled in production). If custom, one might need the appropriate rolling dies for 80 TPI -- which is very fine but not unheard of (optics adjustment screws often are fine pitch rolled). Rolling would yield an excellent surface on the screw. If using Nitronic 60, that too can be rolled (its ductility is good).

### Internal Thread Forming
For the female bronze bushing, one cannot roll an internal thread after the fact, but one can form-tap or use a thread forming process instead of cut tapping. Thread forming taps (also called roll taps) deform the material to create threads without cutting chips. They require a ductile material -- bronze is usually ductile enough for forming, especially phosphor bronze or aluminum bronze in annealed condition. If a form tap is used, the internal thread will likewise be smoother, burr-free, and work-hardened. No metal is removed, so no chips are produced (immediate particle reduction since we don't introduce chips into the part). The formed internal thread also gets work-hardened at the surface. This could improve its galling behavior, similar to rolling. Many industries prefer form-tapped threads for this reason (if tolerances allow the slightly higher tapping torque). So, implementing a formed internal thread in the bronze bushing is recommended if the material and size permit. Phosphor bronze and aluminum bronze can often be form-tapped in fine pitches if lubrication is used during tapping -- since after fabrication the parts will be thoroughly cleaned, using a tap lube in manufacturing is acceptable.

### Thread Fit and Class
Using a slightly looser thread tolerance (e.g. class 2A/2B instead of 3A/3B, or adding a small clearance) can help reduce galling. If threads are too tight, friction and contact area increase. ISO metric standards sometimes deliberately use larger clearance for stainless fasteners to prevent galling. In our case, ensuring a smooth running fit (perhaps not an interference or extremely snug fit) will help. The downside is a bit more backlash in the adjuster, but if kept minimal, the trade-off is worth it to prevent seizure. Given 80 TPI, even a little looseness won't affect positioning much due to the fine pitch.

### Surface Finish Improvements
After thread fabrication (especially if cut), performing a gentle surface treatment like electropolishing (for stainless) or a light polish with an abrasive nylon brush can remove machining marks and burrs. Electropolishing stainless screws removes microscopic high points and can reduce friction. For bronze, a fine wire brushing or passivation dip could smooth it. One must clean any residues after.

### Particle Mitigation
Rolled/formed threads yield far fewer initial particles because there are no cut chips. With cut threads, one should do an ultrasonic cleaning to remove all chips, and possibly a "break-in" outside the clean environment. A break-in procedure might involve assembling the screw and nut a few times (if possible in a controlled way) to wear off the rough initial peaks, then disassembling, cleaning out any debris, and reassembling for actual use. This way, any particles generated at first are removed before the part enters service. This is a common practice for precision actuators (sometimes called run-in or seasoning of threads).

### Strength
Rolled threads are about 10--20% stronger in tension and much stronger in fatigue than cut threads. While our adjuster is not highly loaded in tension, the improved mechanical strength means less likelihood of thread deformation and damage over repeated use.

### Conclusion (Thread Manufacturing)
We strongly recommend rolling the external threads of the screw and using a formed (non-cut) internal thread for the bushing if feasible. This will produce smoother, harder surfaces that resist galling and shed fewer particles. If rolling/forming is not an option, then ensure cut threads are finished to a high quality and cleaned thoroughly. Even in combination with the material/coating solutions above, thread forming is a complementary step -- e.g. a rolled Nitronic 60 screw with a form-tapped phosphor bronze nut would be ideal.

## Summary of Treatments

In order of expected effectiveness for our case, the surface treatments/coatings can be qualitatively ranked as follows:

- **Kolsterising / Low-temp carburizing:** *Outstanding* -- essentially makes 304 behave like Nitronic 60 (or better) with no dimensional change (no galling observed up to yield stress in tests).

- **DLC Coating:** *Outstanding* -- provides a super-hard, ultra-low friction surface (prevents galling and minimizes wear; used for demanding tribology). Needs proper adhesion but is proven in precision tools.

- **Silver Plating:** *Excellent* -- a traditional solution for stainless fasteners in UHV (provides lubricity and anti-seize). Soft but effective within reasonable loads.

- **Electroless Nickel Plating:** *Excellent* -- hard and smooth; plating one side can nearly eliminate galling. More robust than silver in terms of wear, but friction slightly higher than silver.

- **Plasma Nitriding (S-phase):** *Excellent* -- similar outcome to Kolsterising if done properly (very hard surface, low galling; must control process to keep corrosion resistance intact).

- **Thin Dense Chrome:** *Very Good* -- a hard, wear-proof coating; if applied well it can stop galling, but any plating defect could cause issues (chrome flakes are a concern).

- **WS₂/MoS₂ dry lube:** *Very Good (short-term)* -- extremely low friction (galling practically impossible initially), but potential particle issues if the coating wears or sheds. Use only if other options are not feasible due to contamination risk.

- **Wax or PTFE coatings:** (Fluoropolymer anti-seize coatings were mentioned generally in literature) -- *Not suitable for our case*, as they are typically not vacuum-compatible due to outgassing and UV breakdown, so we exclude them even though they help galling in normal settings.

Selecting a combination of material **+** treatment often yields the best result. For instance, a Nitronic 60 screw might not need any coating (already great), whereas a 304 screw could be Kolsterised or DLC-coated to achieve similar anti-galling performance. A bronze nut could be left bare or perhaps nickel-plated if one wanted to harden its surface to reduce wear.

The goal is to achieve a low-friction, high-hardness interface with no reactive materials to contaminate the optics. Most of the above treatments meet that goal.

## Quantifying Galling and Seizing Resistance (Standards & Data)

To compare solutions objectively, galling and friction behavior can be quantified using industry-standard tests:

### ASTM G98 -- Standard Test Method for Galling Resistance
- ASTM G98 is a button-on-block test designed to rank material couples by their resistance to galling. In this test, a cylindrical button is pressed into a flat block of the mating material under a given normal load, then rotated. The load is increased until visible galling occurs. The result is reported as a *threshold galling stress* (the highest stress at which no galling is observed). This test has been used to generate data like that in the Nitronic 60 technical bulletin, which we cited earlier. For example, G98 tests showed Type 304 vs. Type 316 had a threshold of ~21 MPa (3 ksi) -- meaning galling occurred at very low stress. Meanwhile, Nitronic 60 vs. 303 had threshold >300 MPa (50 ksi) -- a huge improvement. We would expect our top solutions to yield similarly high galling thresholds. We could conduct G98 tests on, say, 304+Kolsterising vs. Al-bronze, or Nitronic 60 vs. phosphor bronze. Likely these would not gall even at the maximum test stress (which is often around 50 ksi in many setups). G98 provides a clear quantitative measure to compare material pairs. For instance, if needed, we could say: "Baseline 304 SS + Al-bronze likely has threshold ~100 MPa" (just an estimate, as e.g. Waukesha 88 vs. 303 was >345 MPa with no galling, and 304 vs. 304 was 14 MPa, so 304 vs. bronze is between). After treatment, 304 (Kolsterised) vs. bronze had no galling up to 842 MPa in Bodycote's test -- far beyond operational stress." Using G98 results like "no galling at 122 ksi contact stress after Kolsterising" is a powerful quantitative testament.

### ASTM G133 -- Linearly Reciprocating Sliding Wear
- This standard measures sliding wear by rubbing a ball on a flat with a reciprocating motion under load for a set number of cycles. It can be adapted to test material/coating pairs for wear rate and friction coefficient. For example, we could simulate thread sliding by having a pin of the screw material slide on a flat of the nut material for many cycles and measure volume loss (wear) and friction. G133 would yield a quantitative wear rate (volume lost per distance) and a coefficient of friction. A low wear rate and low friction indicate good performance. If we tested baseline materials, we might see high friction (~0.4--0.5 dry) and significant wear volume. Testing a coated or alternate material pair would show dramatically lower wear. For instance, a DLC-coated steel vs. bronze might show >10× less volume loss than uncoated. We could also observe if friction spikes at any point (which might indicate galling onset). While G133 is not a direct thread test, it helps quantify improvements in sliding behavior due to materials or coatings.

### ISO 16047 -- Threaded Fastener Torque/Clamp Testing
ISO 16047 is a standard to measure the torque-tension relationship of threaded fasteners. It measures how much torque is needed to achieve a certain preload, which is governed by the thread and bearing surface friction. For our purposes, performing a series of tighten-loosen cycles under ISO 16047 conditions can evaluate friction coefficients of threads (the "nut factor" or K-factor) and also reveal any galling. For example, we could install the 1/4-80 screw into a mating part with a known torque and see what axial force (preload) results. A galling-prone combination might show erratic behavior or seize during the test. A galling-resistant one will show consistent torque-tension behavior across multiple cycles. ISO 16047 typically applies to coarser fasteners, but we could adapt it by measuring the torque needed to achieve a certain advancement or using a spring to measure force. One useful outcome is determination of the nut factor *K* (which combines thread friction and head friction into one coefficient). A lower K indicates lower friction. Silver-plated or lubricated threads often have K ~0.15, while dry steel threads can have K ~0.3 or higher. For instance, silver plating on stainless can roughly halve the friction coefficient of the threads. If we see K drop from ~0.25 (baseline dry) to ~0.10 with a given treatment, we've quantitatively improved the interface.

### ASTM G99 -- Pin-on-Disk Wear Test
Another tribology test (pin-on-disk) that can gauge wear resistance of coatings or materials. Not explicitly needed here since G133 covers similar ground, but G99 is commonly used to compare wear rates and friction for coatings.

### ASTM G195 -- Galling Resistance of Fasteners
ASTM G195 is a standard test for galling of fasteners (especially for testing fastener coatings). This involves repeated tightening of a nut on a bolt under load and inspecting for damage. It's akin to a functional galling test. For fine precision threads, more controlled lab tests like G98 are easier to compare materials scientifically, but G195 would be a direct validation of thread performance in realistic conditions.

### ASTM E595 -- Outgassing Test
While not about galling, we could require that any proposed coating meets ASTM E595 (NASA outgassing standard) for use in vacuum optics. For example, DLC, silver, etc., should have Total Mass Loss (TML) <1% and Collected Volatile Condensable Material (CVCM) <0.1%. Most of these inorganic coatings have TML ~0, CVCM ~0 (they have no volatiles). Krytox grease, by contrast, would fail CVCM in UV because it breaks down and deposits. Ensuring all materials pass such standards is important for optical contamination. All our recommendations involve metals or ceramic-like carbon, which essentially have no outgassing.

By applying these standards, we can quantify improvements. For example, a summary could be:

- **Baseline (304 SS + Al-bronze):** Galling observed in functional tests by ~0.5--1.0 N·m torque; G98 threshold est. ~50--100 MPa; friction coefficient ~0.3; visible debris after 10 cycles.

- **Nitronic 60 + Phosphor Bronze:** No galling in functional test up to 5--10 N·m; G98 threshold >345 MPa (no galling at test limit); friction ~0.25; negligible wear after 20 cycles.

- **304 (Kolsterised) + Al-bronze:** No galling up to 5--10 N·m (thread fails mechanically before galling); G98 threshold >>300 MPa (exceeds material yield; essentially no galling in test); friction ~0.25; virtually no wear (just slight polishing of bronze).

- **304 with DLC + Al-bronze:** No galling; friction extremely low (~0.1); wear minimal (only bronze burnishing).

- **304 with Silver Plate + Al-bronze:** No galling; friction low (~0.15); silver transfer film observed, but no seizure.

These are hypothetical values but supported by qualitative data. The key point is that with the right combination, galling can be entirely avoided, whereas the wrong combination fails early -- and these standards help confirm that.

**Thermal Expansion and Stress Considerations at 25 °C**

The operating environment is constant 25 °C with inert gas, so large temperature swings are not present. However, differences in thermal expansion (CTE) between materials could influence fit or stress if not considered:

- **304 SS CTE:** ~17.3×10⁻⁶/°C (at 20 °C).

- **Aluminum Bronze CTE:** ~16--17×10⁻⁶/°C (depends on composition, but close to steel; e.g. nickel-aluminum bronze is ~17×10⁻⁶/°C).

- **Phosphor Bronze CTE:** ~18×10⁻⁶/°C (copper ~17, tin ~22, mixture around 18).

- **Nitronic 60 CTE:** similar to 304 (~16--17×10⁻⁶).

- **17-4 PH CTE:** ~11×10⁻⁶ (H900 condition) -- lower because martensitic.

- **431 SS CTE:** ~10--11×10⁻⁶ (like other martensitics, lower than austenitic).

- **Silver CTE:** ~19×10⁻⁶; **Nickel CTE:** ~13×10⁻⁶; **DLC/graphite CTE:** ~3--5×10⁻⁶ (much lower, but thin coatings don't cause bulk issues).

Given the assembly stays at roughly room temperature, thermal expansion mismatch is minor. The 304 screw and bronze nut in the baseline have very similar CTE (~17 vs ~17), so they expand virtually together -- no differential expansion stress. If we change materials:

- **Nitronic 60 with bronze:** both ~17, no issue.

- **304 with phosphor bronze:** 17 vs 18, negligible difference over a small temperature range.

- **304 with 431 SS nut:** 17 vs 11, that's a difference, but since temperature is constant in operation, it's only initial assembly at a potentially different temperature that might matter. If the system is assembled at 20 °C and runs at 25 °C, a stainless screw will expand slightly more than a 431 nut (which could *increase* clearance by a tiny amount, because the screw's OD grows slightly more than the nut's ID). A 5 °C delta on a 6 mm diameter screw with ΔCTE ~6×10⁻⁶ yields ~0.00018 mm (0.18 µm) differential expansion -- utterly negligible for thread fit.

- If a combination had the opposite mismatch (nut expands more), e.g. if we used an aluminum alloy nut (CTE ~23) with a steel screw (17), then heating could loosen fit. But we are not using aluminum.

- If one part were ceramic (~8) and the other steel (17), a temperature rise might cause one to bind more, but not relevant here.

At constant 25 °C, essentially no thermal strain is introduced. So thermal expansion mismatch is not a factor for galling or loosening in this design.

### Stress-Corrosion Cracking (SCC) Risks
SCC requires a susceptible material, tensile stress, and a corrosive environment (often chlorides for stainless, ammonia for copper, etc.). Here we have:

- 304 SS: susceptible to chloride SCC in the presence of chlorides and stress, but in pure dry N₂ with no moisture, there are practically no corrosive species. No chlorine, no oxygen (so even general corrosion is nil). Thus 304 is safe from SCC in this environment.

- Aluminum bronze: can suffer "season cracking" (ammonia-induced stress corrosion) if stressed and exposed to ammonia. But again, no ammonia or moisture in the purge gas. Pure N₂ means no SCC trigger for bronze.

- Phosphor bronze: similarly could get ammonia SCC, but inert environment means no.

- High-strength 17-4 PH or 431: These can be subject to hydrogen embrittlement or chloride SCC if not properly baked/handled, but no source of H₂ or Cl⁻ is present in operation. (If we plate with chrome or nickel, we must bake to remove hydrogen to avoid embrittlement, but that's a one-time process control, not an in-service risk.)

- If using silver plating, one might consider corrosion if ozone or sulfur were present, but in N₂, silver will just mildly tarnish over a long time if any sulfur outgasses from other components (which shouldn't happen in a clean system). Tarnish isn't structural or SCC -- silver doesn't undergo SCC.

So stress-corrosion is essentially a non-issue at 25 °C in an inert atmosphere. Each candidate material is stable in that environment:

- Austenitic stainless retains its passive oxide (there's no O₂ to grow it, but also no stimuli to break it down).

- Bronzes just sit inert (no oxygen to form cupric oxide).

- Even if trace O₂ got in, it would form a protective oxide on bronze, not cause cracking.

**Galvanic Corrosion:** With dissimilar metals (steel/bronze) in the presence of an electrolyte, galvanic corrosion could occur. But again, dry N₂ means no electrolyte (no moisture), so galvanic coupling between stainless and bronze is not a concern inside the chamber. If there were humidity, stainless-bronze is actually a mild couple (stainless is noble, bronze slightly less, but usually not severe). In any case, for completeness: ensure no condensation or water enters. Otherwise one might have to consider if plating introduces galvanic issues (e.g. a noble silver coating on steel in presence of saltwater could accelerate steel corrosion). But since everything is either noble (silver, gold) or protected by inert environment, we are safe.

**Mechanical Stresses:** The threads will see mechanical stress from tightening. Fine threads have less thread cross-section, but our loads are small (just positioning an optic). There is likely no risk of mechanical yield or fatigue in normal use. (However, if someone over-tightened to the point of yielding the threads, that itself can cause galling -- but that's misuse, and our recommendations on torque will address it.)

Thus, from a thermal and environmental standpoint, all proposed solutions are compatible and stable at 25 °C in N₂, with no notable expansion mismatch or corrosion concerns. The only caution is to properly post-treat plated parts to avoid hydrogen embrittlement (baking plated high-strength parts). 304 and bronzes are not high-strength enough to worry about hydrogen embrittlement, but if we nitrided or carburized, there could be some residual stress (still, those processes usually conclude with a slow cool that relieves excessive stress).

In conclusion, thermal and SCC factors do not constrain our material/coating choices -- this gives us the freedom to pick optimal tribological solutions without worry of these failure modes.

# Comparison of Top Solutions and Rankings

Bringing together the above analyses, we can rank the top-performing material + treatment solutions against the criteria of galling resistance, wear/particle generation, and contamination risk. We also factor in practicality and cost. **Table 2** below summarizes several leading candidates:

**Nitronic 60 screw / Phosphor Bronze nut (no coating):**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Excellent.** No galling in expected use. Nitronic 60 resists adhesion up to very high stress; bronze provides a dissimilar interface -- galling is virtually impossible under normal loads.

- *Wear & Particle Generation:* **Minimal wear.** Bronze may experience slight smoothing wear; Nitronic remains unscathed. Very few particles (mostly a fine bronze oxide dust, if any).

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.** All-metal, no volatiles. Vacuum compatible.

- *Approx. Cost:* **Medium.** Nitronic alloy + bronze ~2--3× material cost of 304, machining is slightly harder (more tool wear), but no special processes needed.

**304 SS screw (Kolsterised) / Aluminum Bronze nut:**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Excellent.** Galling eliminated. Hardened 304 surface (>1000 HV) does not gall even above yield stress. Bronze cannot gall the hard surface.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Minimal wear.** Bronze will undergo minor wear against the super-hard stainless. No adhesive transfer; particles extremely few (tiny bronze rubbings).

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.** Treated layer is diffusion-based (no coating). No outgassing or flaking.

- *Approx. Cost:* **High.** Proprietary process cost (batch treatment); uses standard materials but requires sending out for treatment. Per-part cost can be significant unless done in batch.

**304 SS screw (DLC coated) / Phosphor Bronze nut:**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Excellent.** DLC's low friction and extreme hardness prevent galling entirely. No metal-metal adhesion occurs.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Very low wear.** DLC (~9 GPa hardness) won't appreciably wear in 20 cycles; bronze will just polish slightly. Few if any particles (maybe some microscopic bronze debris).

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.** DLC is inert carbon, no outgassing. Must ensure no flaking (with good adhesion there will be none).

- *Approx. Cost:* **High.** DLC coating adds cost (tens of dollars each in small qty). Requires professional coating service (batch processing).

**304 SS screw (Silver plated) / Aluminum Bronze nut:**

- *Galling Resistance:* **High.** Extremely low galling tendency. Silver lubricates the interface, preventing seizure. Galling unlikely unless plating is damaged.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Low wear.** Initial cycles smear the silver; after that, the interface is essentially silver-on-bronze (soft on soft) with low friction. Some silver flakes may shed early, but once seated, few particles are generated.

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Mostly clean.** No outgassing (pure metal). Need to remove any loose silver particles after assembly (e.g. by gentle wiping or air blow-off). Tarnish over time is possible but not harmful (and doesn't happen in pure N₂).

- *Approx. Cost:* **Medium.** Plating cost is modest (silver plating is on the order of 1--2× the cost of the screw). Requires handling to avoid scratching the plating.

**Nitronic 60 screw / Nitronic 60 nut (both rolled):**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Excellent.** This alloy pair endures high loads with no galling. 20 adjustments are well within safe range with no adhesion.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Very low wear.** Both parts are galling-resistant and strong. They may polish each other slightly, generating negligible debris.

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.** All-metal, no coatings or lubricants. Nitronic's oxide is stable in N₂, no outgassing.

- *Approx. Cost:* **Medium.** Material cost ~2--3× 304 for both parts; machining is tougher. No extra processing steps, though.

**304 SS screw (EN nickel plated) / Phosphor Bronze nut:**

- *Galling Resistance:* **High.** Hard nickel-phosphorus coating prevents adhesion. Galling very unlikely unless the coating is somehow penetrated.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Low wear.** The nickel coating (500+ HV) will take the wear; the bronze or steel counterpart will see reduced friction. Some wear of the nickel is possible over many cycles, producing a little Ni dust, but the coating should hold for 20 cycles.

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.** Electroless Ni is a metal alloy -- no volatile components once plated and baked. Must rinse thoroughly after plating to remove chemicals. A well-bonded coating won't flake.

- *Approx. Cost:* **Low--Medium.** Plating is relatively inexpensive per part. It adds a processing step but not high cost (nickel plating small parts in batch might be only a couple dollars each).

**304 SS screw / 431 SS nut (both hard, rolled):**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Medium-High.* Hard 431 vs. softer 304 reduces galling tendency. Should withstand moderate tightening without galling, especially with slow adjustments.

- *Wear & Particles:* **Moderate wear.* The 304 male may experience some micro-galling incipients (small shear spots) against 431 if over-torqued. Could produce fine stainless dust. If kept in proper torque range, wear is minor.

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.* No coatings, just stainless steel. No outgassing. Any wear debris is stainless (which could potentially be attracted to optics via static). Not as ideal as above options in terms of particle cleanliness.

- *Approx. Cost:* **Low.* Material is readily available; just requires heat treat for 431. Low additional cost beyond standard machining.

**304 SS screw (uncoated) / Phosphor Bronze nut:**

- *Galling Resistance:* **Medium.* Dissimilar pairing gives moderate galling resistance. Likely no galling if torque is low and adjustments are limited, but not foolproof (some risk if adjustment is aggressive or many cycles).

- *Wear & Particles:* **Fair wear.* Bronze will act as the sacrificial metal; some bronze particles or powder may form over repeated use, though less severe than stainless-on-stainless.

- *Outgassing / Cleanliness:* **Clean.* No volatiles, but copper oxide particles could deposit on optics (and under UV could darken). Manageable with careful use and perhaps shielding the area.

- *Approx. Cost:* **Low.* Very simple materials, low cost (baseline-like cost, just using Ph bronze instead of Al bronze).

*Table 2: Performance ranking of top material/treatment solutions.* All options assume high-quality thread fabrication (rolled/form & cleaned). Galling resistance is qualitatively rated relative to the baseline (baseline 304+bronze was poor). Wear/particle notes consider ~20 adjustment cycles in dry N₂.

From the above, we see multiple viable approaches to meet the goals. The absolute top performers in terms of galling and cleanliness are those combining a galling-proof alloy or treatment with a dissimilar counterpart: e.g. Nitronic 60 with bronze, Kolsterised 304 with bronze, or DLC-coated 304 with bronze. These essentially eliminate the root cause of galling and generate practically no debris. They do come with higher cost or processing complexity. Slightly below are simpler solutions like silver plating or using Nitronic 60 for both parts -- these still give excellent performance, with perhaps a bit more wear in the case of silver (since it's soft and can wear) or a bit more friction in the case of Nitronic-on-Nitronic (which, though it doesn't gall, isn't as low-friction as a lubricated interface).

The more middle-ground solutions (431/304, or just 304 vs a good bronze without coatings) can work with caution but don't provide the same margin of safety -- they reduce the likelihood of galling but not to zero. Given the severity of failure (contaminating optics and potentially ruining them), the top-tier solutions are justified despite higher cost.

**Cost Analysis of Top Solutions**

While performance is paramount in a high-precision optical system, cost and practical implementation must be considered. Below we outline the cost implications of the top candidates:

**Baseline (304/Al-Bronze, no coating):** This is the lowest cost in materials and processes. 304 stainless and aluminum bronze are common; manufacturing involved standard machining of threads. However, the cost of failure was high (downtime, optic contamination, etc.). Thus, the baseline "cheap" solution proved to be false economy. Even a modest cost increase to prevent failure is easily justified when considering the expense of cleaning or replacing optics and the labor involved in dealing with seized components. As noted by Meyer Tool, the time and effort spent trying to remove seized fasteners or to repair damaged threads can easily dwarf the cost of some specialty fasteners or a tube of anti-seize [@meyer2019]. In other words, investing in galling prevention yields a lower total cost of ownership in such systems.

**Nitronic 60 Screw / Bronze Nut:** *Material cost:* Nitronic 60 is about $15--20 per kg (vs $5--7/kg for 304), so raw material is ~3×, but for a small screw this difference is negligible in absolute terms (pennies more). Machining Nitronic is a bit slower (more tool wear), adding perhaps 20--30% more machining cost for the screw. Bronze nut material cost is similar to aluminum bronze (maybe $10--15/kg).
